If Harvard University hired me to prepare their students for the future of work, here's exactly what I'd focus on ⇢

The world is changing faster than most curricula can keep up with.

Students are graduating into a workforce that looks nothing like the one their professors prepared for.

Here's my proposal:

𝐒𝐭𝐞𝐩 𝟏: 𝐑𝐞𝐥𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬𝐡𝐢𝐩 𝐁𝐮𝐢𝐥𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 & 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐀𝐫𝐭 𝐨𝐟 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐈𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐯𝐢𝐞𝐰

Most students know how to apply for a job. Very few know how to make someone remember them.

⇢ The ability to connect genuinely with an interviewer is irreplaceable. It will get you further than any "Easy Apply" button ever will.

⇢ The most powerful professional networks aren't built at formal events. They're built at coffee shops, dinner tables, and chance conversations. Students who understand this will never have to rely on a job board.

⇢ The ability to communicate clearly, confidently, and professionally is becoming increasingly rare. Students who can write a thoughtful email, hold a real conversation, and present their ideas without defaulting to a screen will stand out immediately.

𝐒𝐭𝐞𝐩 𝟐: 𝐏𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐨𝐧𝐚𝐥 𝐁𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠

Your degree gets you considered. Your personal brand gets you a contract.

⇢ A resume is no longer enough. Hiring managers are looking at your digital presence before they ever pick up the phone.

⇢ The ability to read a room and hold your own without being the loudest person is an underrated career skill no one is formally teaching.

⇢ The competition isn't just other graduates anymore. It's automation. Students who invest in their personal brand will always be more than a resume in a pile.

𝐒𝐭𝐞𝐩 𝟑: 𝐀𝐝𝐚𝐩𝐭𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲 & 𝐀𝐈

The most valuable skill a new graduate can have right now isn't a specific degree/specialization.

It's the ability to pivot, learn fast, and stay relevant as everything shifts.

⇢ Every industry is changing. The ability to learn new skills quickly and move forward without losing confidence is no longer optional. It is the expectation.

⇢ Using AI is the bare minimum. However, AI is a tool, not a replacement. The students who learn to use it as a collaborator while keeping their human edge sharp will be the ones companies fight to hire.

⇢Students who treat change as an opportunity instead of a threat will outlast everyone who is waiting for things to go back to normal. They won't.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝘁𝘂𝗱𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗼 𝗴𝗿𝗮𝗱𝘂𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘀𝗲 𝘀𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹𝘀 𝘄𝗼𝗻'𝘁 𝗯𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗿𝗼𝘄𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗿é𝘀𝘂𝗺é𝘀 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘃𝗼𝗶𝗱 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗵𝗼𝗽𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗸𝘀.

𝙏𝙝𝙚𝙮'𝙡𝙡 𝙗𝙚 𝙩𝙝𝙚 𝙤𝙣𝙚𝙨 𝙘𝙝𝙤𝙤𝙨𝙞𝙣𝙜 𝙗𝙚𝙩𝙬𝙚𝙚𝙣 𝙤𝙛𝙛𝙚𝙧𝙨.

Harvard — my DMs are open. 😊

Stop hiring more people to solve your "messy operations" problem.

Adding headcount to a broken process doesn’t fix the process—it just makes the mess more expensive.
In a high-stakes environment like Legal or SaaS, "Execution Friction" is the silent killer of profit.
A missed client follow-up isn't just an admin error. It’s a formal complaint waiting to happen.

I help firms build "Digital Employees" using AI and n8n that:
✅ Monitor every contract expiry.
✅ Automate client intake and routing.
✅ Ensure 100% compliance audit trails.
Don't scale your chaos. Automate it.

Happy Monday, everyone! 

I’ll be honest, I’ve been through a few interview cycles lately, and I still haven’t found my next "home." It’s a vulnerable thing to admit publicly, but I’ve always believed in being real about the journey, not just posting about the curated highlights of my career.  

My current contract at Meta was recently extended through the end of May. It was a nice vote of confidence because the work I’ve been doing there really mattered to the team. In a pretty short window, I was able to make an impact across several product areas, and they valued that enough to keep me on a bit longer to help me until my next opportunity comes. That means a lot to me; it just reinforces what I already know: when I’m given an opportunity, I show up, and I deliver.

That said, I’m actively on the hunt for my next UX Research role, whether that’s FTE or contract. I’m based near Dallas, and while I’m prioritizing remote or local opportunities, I’m definitely open to relocating if the fit is right.

Here’s a quick snippet of what I bring to the table: I’m a mixed-methods researcher with experience across fintech, commerce, ads, education, social, and generative AI. I’ve led research at Meta, handled enterprise CRM migration work at Adobe, and built research foundations at GM Financial and FIS. My career actually started in academic librarianship, which gave me a bit of a "secret weapon" in this field: a deep foundation in information architecture and knowledge management that I bring into every single project.

I also have a Doctorate in Leadership, with a focus on Emotional Intelligence. That heavily shapes how I partner with teams, how I talk to stakeholders, and how I think about people behind the data points. I'm genuinely excited about the intersection of AI and research. I utilize tools like Claude and NotebookLM in my daily workflow because I am committed to understanding what rigorous, human-centered research looks like as our tools evolve.

🪪 DOCUMENT SAFETY IS BECOMING A FRONTIER AI PROBLEM

DocShield caught my attention because it tackles a problem that is going to get much larger very quickly.

As generative systems improve, so does the ability to create realistic text-heavy document forgeries. That means the next safety wave is not only about chat outputs or image deepfakes. It is about invoices, contracts, IDs, forms, and paperwork that people and institutions still trust by default.

This paper uses evidence-grounded agentic reasoning, which is exactly the kind of direction that makes sense. Safety systems that only look for surface cues will keep losing to better generation. Verification has to become more evidential, contextual, and explainable.

That is the bigger point.

In many categories, the value in AI will shift from generation to verification. Whoever can prove something is genuine, traceable, and trustworthy may end up with the stronger long-term position than whoever can generate the most convincing fake.

That is not a side market.

That is core infrastructure for a world full of synthetic media.

Which category do you think gets hit hardest first by AI document fraud: finance, hiring, insurance, or government services?

What if I told you Romania might be the best-kept secret in your hiring playbook for 2026—and not just because of cost savings. A new article I’ve just published explains why tech leaders should consider outsourcing to Romania this year. It’s called *“Software Outsourcing to Romania in 2026: Rates, Talent Pool, and What CTOs Need to Know.”*

Here’s the insight you’ll want to carry forward: Romania delivers above-average engineering quality while undercutting Western European and U.S. costs by 30-50%. It’s not just about juniors. Mid-level software developers typically charge between USD 25-45/hour, while senior specialists and niche roles push into the USD 45-75 range.

The tech workforce in Romania is no longer emerging—it’s over 190,000 strong, with universities pumping out 9,000-10,000 qualified graduates yearly, complemented by mature communities in AI, cybersecurity, embedded software, fintech, and enterprise systems. The strength lies as much in numbers as in seniority: the mid-to-senior segment now commands greater share of demand, pushing up average rates—but also delivering more predictable outcomes.

For CTOs thinking strategic outsourcing: location still matters within Romania. Bucharest, Cluj-Napoca, and Timișoara lead on rates and seniority, with Iași offering a more cost-efficient but still strong alternative. Choose the right mix of city, seniority, and tech stack—Java, Python, .NET remain common; AI, DevOps, blockchain are premiums.

If balancing budget with risk, a pilot-resented team or staff augmentation in Romania can prove the value before committing to long-term contracts. Also verify English fluency, retention data, and how turnover is handled locally—it often separates good providers from costly regrets. 💼 INDIA'S IT SECTOR IS SHOWING AI'S TRANSITION COSTS

A lot of AI commentary is still framed like this: productivity up, margins up, everyone wins later.

Reality is messier.

The Reuters report on Indian IT firms points to a more difficult middle phase, where clients worry about demand, pricing, staffing, and the pace of AI-driven change all at once. That matters because services businesses sit very close to the place where technology hype meets operating reality.

When customers think AI can reduce effort, they start asking why they are still paying yesterday's rates. Even before full automation arrives, pricing pressure arrives. That is the transition cost many markets underestimate.

This is why AI disruption will not only show up as sudden job losses. It will also show up as squeezed contracts, slower hiring, and a tougher negotiation climate for labour-intensive firms.

The broad lesson is simple: AI changes bargaining power before it changes the org chart.

That is a much earlier signal.

Which sectors do you think will feel that pricing pressure first: IT services, consulting, legal work, or marketing agencies?

Enterprise AI: Take a Pause (a longer pause now ) - World will never be the same after all these ongoing Wars !!!!! 

We’re in a rush to adopt AI, but macro shocks — wars, geopolitical shifts, supply‑chain disruptions, regulatory change — are creating prolonged volatility that will reshape markets, talent, and technology. Before accelerating AI projects, take a longer, deliberate pause to stabilize your technology and business environment so AI can be sustainable and resilient.

What to prioritize now
- Business continuity: update BCP/DR plans for extended regional disruptions and supply‑chain impacts.
- Infrastructure resilience: validate multi‑region/multi‑cloud failover, capacity planning, and cost controls for sustained stress.
- Telecom & network robustness: diversify carriers, test failovers, and instrument end‑to‑end performance.
- Cybersecurity posture: re‑verify identity, access, vendor risk, and incident response under hostile scenarios.
- Applications & SaaS stability: map dependencies, enforce API resilience, and secure data portability.
- Talent & staffing plans: cross‑train teams, document critical runbooks, and plan for longer hiring/retention cycles.
- Financial stress testing: model budget shocks, inflated costs, and slower ROI timelines for AI initiatives.
- Regulatory & legal readiness: anticipate stricter data, export, and vendor‑control rules; update contracts and compliance playbooks.
- Sustainability & ethical risk: evaluate long‑term environmental and societal impacts of AI choices under constrained resources.
- Observable SLOs & decision gates: require infrastructure and business resilience gates before greenlighting AI rollouts.

Practical next steps (short timeline)
- 30 days: run a resilience workshop; map top 10 risks and single points of failure across tech and business.
- 60 days: implement failover for highest‑risk services, add monitoring/SLOs, and start cross‑team drills.
- 90 days: complete critical vendor contingency plans, update contracts, and validate recovery playbooks for business‑critical AI features.

Decision checkpoint before any new AI approval
“If a major geopolitical or supply‑chain shock persists for months, can this AI initiative continue to operate without jeopardizing core business continuity, compliance, or financial stability?” If not, extend the pause and stabilize first.

Qasar Younis (CEO) & Peter Ludwig (CTO) of Applied Intuition join Sourcery to break down how they’ve quietly built one of the most important AI companies in the world reaching a $15 billion valuation.

Founded in 2017, Applied Intuition is building the infrastructure layer for vehicle intelligence and physical AI, powering systems across automotive, defense, trucking, construction, mining, and agriculture. Today, 18 of the top 20 global automakers and major U.S. Department of Defense programs rely on their software.

The company has raised $1B+ in total funding, including a $600M Series F at a $15B valuation, co-led by BlackRock and Kleiner Perkins, with participation from global investors such as Franklin Templeton, Qatar Investment Authority, Abu Dhabi Investment Council (ADIC), Premji Invest, Stripes, Greycroft, BAM Elevate, and 137 Ventures.

Existing investors include Fidelity Management & Research Company, General Catalyst, Lux Capital, BOND, Elad Gil, Addition, and Tribe Capital, alongside early backing from Marc Andreessen.

What makes the company especially unique: despite raising over $1B, Applied Intuition has largely not relied on that capital to fund operations, instead building a profitable, capital-efficient business with long-term customer contracts and deep integration across industries

In this conversation, we cover:

• Why physical AI is fundamentally different from software AI
• How breakthroughs like transformers unlocked real-world autonomy
• The company’s horizontal-first strategy across industries
• Why they stayed quiet for years—and why that changed
• How they deploy autonomy in defense and industrial environments
• What hiring looks like in the AI era

Applied Intuition isn’t building another AI app—it’s building the infrastructure for AI in the real world.

Enterprise AI: Take a Pause (a longer pause now ) !!!!

We’re in a rush to adopt AI, but macro shocks — wars, geopolitical shifts, supply‑chain disruptions, regulatory change — are creating prolonged volatility that will reshape markets, talent, and technology. Before accelerating AI projects, take a longer, deliberate pause to stabilize your technology and business environment so AI can be sustainable and resilient.

What to prioritize now
- Business continuity: update BCP/DR plans for extended regional disruptions and supply‑chain impacts.
- Infrastructure resilience: validate multi‑region/multi‑cloud failover, capacity planning, and cost controls for sustained stress.
- Telecom & network robustness: diversify carriers, test failovers, and instrument end‑to‑end performance.
- Cybersecurity posture: re‑verify identity, access, vendor risk, and incident response under hostile scenarios.
- Applications & SaaS stability: map dependencies, enforce API resilience, and secure data portability.
- Talent & staffing plans: cross‑train teams, document critical runbooks, and plan for longer hiring/retention cycles.
- Financial stress testing: model budget shocks, inflated costs, and slower ROI timelines for AI initiatives.
- Regulatory & legal readiness: anticipate stricter data, export, and vendor‑control rules; update contracts and compliance playbooks.
- Sustainability & ethical risk: evaluate long‑term environmental and societal impacts of AI choices under constrained resources.
- Observable SLOs & decision gates: require infrastructure and business resilience gates before greenlighting AI rollouts.

Practical next steps (short timeline)
- 30 days: run a resilience workshop; map top 10 risks and single points of failure across tech and business.
- 60 days: implement failover for highest‑risk services, add monitoring/SLOs, and start cross‑team drills.
- 90 days: complete critical vendor contingency plans, update contracts, and validate recovery playbooks for business‑critical AI features.

Decision checkpoint before any new AI approval
“If a major geopolitical or supply‑chain shock persists for months, can this AI initiative continue to operate without jeopardizing core business continuity, compliance, or financial stability?” If not, extend the pause and stabilize first.

Location-Remote
Mode-Contract

Mandatory Skill: Should have strong experience in recommendation systems and agentic AI
 
Job Description: Recommendation Engineer (Machine Learning)
 
About the role:
We’re looking for a Recommendation Engineer to design, build, and optimize machine learning systems that personalize user experiences across content, products, or services. You’ll work end-to-end—from data and feature engineering to model training, deployment, and experimentation—partnering closely with Product, Data, and Platform teams.
Key Responsibilities:
Design and develop recommendation models using collaborative filtering, content-based methods, and hybridapproaches (including ranking and retrieval architectures).
Build and maintain scalable pipelines for user behavior collection, feature engineering, and batch + near-real-time data processing (e.g., Spark, SQL, streaming).
Train, evaluate, and fine-tune models using modern ML frameworks; productionize training and inference workflows on cloud infrastructure (e.g., managed training, endpoints, CI/CD for ML).
Define success metrics (CTR, engagement, retention, conversion), run A/B tests, and iterate based on experiment outcomes and model monitoring.
Implement model observability: data drift, model drift, bias/fairness checks, and automated retraining/rollback strategies.
Collaborate with engineering teams to integrate recommendation services into APIs, apps, and data products with reliability, latency, and scalability constraints.
Document system design, trade-offs, and model behavior for technical and non-technical stakeholders.
 
Required Qualifications:
3+ years of experience building and shipping ML models in production, ideally personalization or recommender systems.
Strong foundations in machine learning, statistics, and optimization, with practical experience in ranking/recall problems.
Proficiency in Python and strong SQL skills; experience with distributed data processing (e.g., Spark).
Experience deploying models to production (batch or real-time), including model packaging, inference APIs, and monitoring.
Solid understanding of experimentation and causal measurement (A/B testing, guardrail metrics, statistical significance).
Ability to communicate clearly, write design docs, and work cross-functionally.
Preferred Qualifications (Nice to Have)
Experience with large-scale personalization (embeddings, ranking systems, multi-stage recommenders)
Familiarity with ML platforms (e.g., Amazon SageMaker – training, pipelines, deployment)
Experience with cloud (AWS/GCP) and MLOps tools (model tracking, CI/CD, monitoring)
Understanding of privacy, PII handling, and responsible AI
Languages: Python, SQL
Data: Spark, data lakes/warehouses, streaming pipelines
ML: Deep learning frameworks, embeddings, ranking models
MLOps: CI/CD, model registry, experiment tracking, monitoring
Cloud: Managed ML services
